title: Drafting Services

narrative.executive_view: Drafting Services looks like a poor $1–10M EBITDA acquisition despite a large pool of automatable production work. The NAICS definition is detailed layouts, plans, and illustrations made from engineering or architectural specifications [S1], and BLS describes drafters as converting those designs into technical drawings with CAD/BIM tools [S2]. That makes labor displacement potential substantial. But the economic prize is unlikely to remain with a bureau: the market already supports hourly and fixed-per-sheet competition, including an Indian provider publicly offering $8/hour conversion and $25–$75 standard-sheet drafting [S4]. The supplied dataset also finds only nine firms in the target EBITDA band. I therefore assess high V but very weak C, a thin buyable pool, and MELTING terminal demand.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The production task is primarily digital translation, revision, detailing, and documentation from someone else’s specifications. BLS lists CAD plan creation, working from architect/engineer sketches and specifications, adding details, and working under their supervision [S2]. Autodesk already markets AI workflow automation, AutoCAD markup assistance that guides automatic incorporation of markups, and generative design alternatives [S3]. These features do not prove end-to-end autonomous permit-ready drafting, but they directly target repetitive drawing and revision work. I judge CAD drafters and the unlisted residual as most exposed; engineers, architects, managers, and inspectors retain more judgment, client coordination, or physical responsibility. The supplied 5413 role mix is engineering-heavy rather than 541340-specific, so the residual judgment is deliberately explicit rather than pretending the input is a code-level census of the production floor.
narrative.value_capture: Savings are unlikely to be retained for long. The observable price menu in [S4] is already both hourly and fixed-per-drawing, with extremely low offshore reference prices. That is not a representative market survey, but it demonstrates a credible, directly comparable outsourced substitute and a buyer-friendly pricing form. An AI-enabled domestic bureau can reduce hours, yet clients can demand lower per-sheet prices, rebid, or use their own CAD tools; the expected channel is price deflation rather than durable margin expansion. The underlying deliverable follows a client’s engineering or architectural specification [S1][S2], and BLS says drafting certification is not mandatory [S2]. I infer little independent service moat, while separating that inference from the distinct demand question: a human review or client stamp may still be needed without giving the bureau pricing power.
narrative.adoption_timing: I estimate current effective adoption at 20%, below the 50% boundary, and four years to 50% effective adoption in the relevant client/bureau workflows. Autodesk’s current A&E product material shows live AI capabilities but also reports implementation limitations and a technical-skills shortage even as most leaders plan to increase AI investment [S3]. That supports an adoption curve rather than instant replacement. It is not a survey of 541340 firms, so both the current level and timing are low-confidence estimates. The key risk is faster client-side adoption: if design firms use AI-enabled AutoCAD, Revit, or Forma internally, outsourced overflow work can decline before a bureau has time to capture the productivity gain.
narrative.consolidation_economics: Buyability is the more immediate practical problem. The frozen dataset derives only nine firms in the stated EBITDA band out of 2,956 total firms, and its own size-class result is noisy enough to warrant caution. Broad architecture-and-engineering marketplace data are directionally useful but not a drafting-bureau comp: BizBuySell reports 2021–25 sold-business earnings multiples of 1.83–3.15 times seller discretionary earnings, not EBITDA, and median sold-company revenue of about $1.09M [S5]. I apply a discounted estimated 1.5x buy and 1.0x exit EBITDA multiple because this code has a tiny target pool, owner dependence, and worsening demand, but no directly observed code-level EBITDA transaction supports either value. General owner-aging evidence does identify succession friction—51% of responding employer-business owners were 55+ in Census’s 2018 reference data [S6], and Project Equity reported one-third of owners over 50 had difficulty finding a buyer [S7]—but neither is drafting-specific and a succession problem does not create a roll-up market.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING for the core outsourced production-drafting service. The formal industry definition and BLS task description both make the deliverable a translation from upstream engineering or architectural specifications into detailed digital documentation [S1][S2]. AI automation can therefore remove outsourced hours and enable the upstream client to insource, rather than merely cut the bureau’s costs. Possible survivors include difficult legacy conversion, tight client-standard integration, and field-linked as-built or scan-to-BIM work. I found no credible source that quantifies those niches as a share of current 541340 revenue, and field capture/surveying may instead fall in 541370; they should not be treated as a demonstrated terminal-value floor. AI-assisted work still exists in the near term, but the paid independent production layer faces a five-to-ten-year erosion risk.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This is an unusually weak-evidence code. The supplied labor mix is at four-digit 5413 and understates pure drafters; because the finalizer must retain its wage weights, the 48.44% residual carries a large hypothesis. The publicly posted offshore rate card is one provider’s offer, not a transaction dataset, and AI product pages establish capability rather than measured adoption or autonomous drawing quality. Owner-age data are old and all-industry; valuation data are broad A&E seller-discretionary-earnings transactions, not 541340 EBITDA deals. Conversely, those limitations could understate valuable specialist niches where drawing context, proprietary standards, rapid coordination, and liability-sensitive QA preserve a customer relationship. The packet deliberately does not quantify those niches without evidence, and its low confidence should be treated as a reason for deal-level diligence, not precision.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System — Sector 54 archive
sources.what_it_supports: Defines 541340 as establishments drawing detailed layouts, plans, and illustrations from engineering and architectural specifications; also distinguishes surveying and mapping activity.

sources.title: Drafters — Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.what_it_supports: Describes drafters’ CAD/BIM-based work, including converting architect and engineer designs to technical drawings, working from specifications, and the fact that certification is not mandatory.

sources.title: AI in Architecture & Engineering
sources.what_it_supports: Shows current Autodesk A&E AI workflow capabilities, including AutoCAD markup assistance, generative design, and management’s stated implementation limitations and planned AI investment.

sources.title: Pricing — Outsource CAD Conversion / Drafting Services Delhi
sources.what_it_supports: A live provider rate card offering fixed-price and hourly CAD services, including stated $8/hour conversion, sheet-level prices, and architectural/MEP drafting work from India.

sources.title: Architecture & Engineering Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.what_it_supports: Reports broad architecture-and-engineering marketplace transaction trends, 2021–25 seller-discretionary-earnings multiples, and the distinction between SDE/cash-flow multiples and revenue multiples.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.what_it_supports: Census visualization using 2018 Annual Business Survey data stating that 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older, with documented coverage limitations.

sources.title: 2.3 Million Small Businesses Nationwide Owned by Aging Boomers Preparing To Retire
sources.what_it_supports: Reports general small-business succession friction, including a statement that one-third of owners over 50 report difficulty finding a buyer; its cited data are not drafting-specific and use older Census inputs.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541340 ($693,287,501) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541340 ($1,675,531,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $182,559 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541340); class <5: 2,510 firms, avg revenue/firm $232,175 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 257 firms, avg revenue/firm $967,078 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $151,348 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,459,411 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $384,898 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,149,234 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $962,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,641,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,386,921 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,164,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $964,666 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 9
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: S1 and S2 establish a digital production workflow from upstream specifications, while S3 documents currently available workflow automation. Fractions are task judgments for the immutable supplied roles, not observed 541340 automation measurements. The unlisted residual is judged drafting/technician/admin-heavy because the supplied 5413 mix is acknowledged to be unrepresentative of this six-digit industry.
caveats: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5413 rather than 541340 and must remain untouched; it likely underrepresents drafting production roles.
The 48.44% residual is the largest judgment and is not a reweighted industry estimate.
Sources demonstrate workflow and software capability, not observed task-level automation rates for 541340.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Civil engineers retain design and accountable engineering judgment; CAD documentation, revisions, and some analysis support can be automated.
Architects retain client, aesthetic, and professional judgment, while routine alternatives, markups, and documentation are AI-assisted.
Scheduling, transmittals, status summaries, and document coordination are automatable, but client and exception management remain human-led.
Architectural and civil drafters perform the CAD-based detailed drawing and revision work directly targeted by automation; human QA and unusual details remain.
Operations management retains selling, accountability, and escalations, but production planning, reporting, and workflow management can be reduced.
Mechanical engineers retain design authority and technical review; repetitive layout, alternatives, and documentation can be automated.
Architectural and engineering managers retain client, staffing, and approval accountability, while reporting and production coordination can be automated.
Physical building inspection and site observation are much less replaceable; document intake and reporting are the limited automatable share.
Electrical engineers retain accountable design and review; standardized layouts, checks, and documentation are partially automatable.
Software development retains architecture and integration work, but code generation, testing, and documentation are substantially AI-assisted.

Because the four-digit mix omits much of a drafting bureau’s expected production and administrative work, the residual is estimated as predominantly digital drafting, checking, file handling, and administration, with some human QA.

pi_dist.rationale: The live provider menu uses hourly and fixed-per-sheet pricing and offers low offshore rates. I infer that a domestic bureau retains only a small fraction of an AI-driven production-cost saving before rebids, client insourcing, or price-per-sheet deflation pass it through.
caveats: S4 is one offshore provider’s offer, not a representative transaction or pricing survey.
The selected value is a competitive pass-through judgment; it does not assume adoption speed or terminal demand.

pi_moat.rationale: The code’s stated output is a drawing from upstream specifications, BLS describes work under engineers or architects, and drafting certification is not mandatory. I infer limited operator-specific switching cost or exclusive right after controlling for distribution; specialized standards and client coordination prevent a zero value.
caveats: These sources describe work and certification, not a measured pricing-moat survey or legal analysis of every state and client contract.
Human review may preserve demand but is not counted here as a capture moat.

t50_years.rationale: Available A&E AI features establish a practical starting point, while Autodesk reports implementation limitations and skills constraints. I estimate four years for 50% effective adoption in relevant client/bureau production workflows, not for all AI use.
caveats: S3 is a vendor source and gives no NAICS 541340 adoption measurement.
This adoption estimate is independent of target count, owner age, or consolidation.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Live AI features and investment intent indicate meaningful but not majority effective use in production drafting workflows; the 20% value is a conservative judgment below the 50% threshold.
caveats: No source located reports current effective adoption specifically for US 541340 operators or their clients.
Product availability is not equivalent to production-quality adoption.

historical_analogs.value: Existing offshore CAD competition is the closest observed analog: a live provider sells standardized drawing work hourly and per sheet, indicating that a digital, specification-driven deliverable has already faced tradable production-price pressure. It is not a measured historical AI-adoption study.
historical_analogs.rationale: The rate card supplies a concrete analog for price competition in the same class of work; the mapping from offshore competition to AI adoption is an explicit judgment.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Weak-to-moderate analogy; it supports pass-through direction, not a numerical adoption rate.
caveats: One provider’s price menu is not a time series, market share estimate, or causal proof of AI-related deflation.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were 55+ in 2018. With no 541340 or 60+ split, I use 35% as a broad, deliberately imprecise 60+ proxy rather than treating the 55+ statistic as exact.
caveats: The source is 2018 all-industry responding employer businesses, not 541340, the $1–10M EBITDA band, or a current 60+ count.
This general demographic proxy is used only for buyability, not adoption or value capture.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Project Equity documents general small-business buyer difficulty, reporting that one-third of owners over 50 had difficulty finding a buyer. I treat that as documentation of a broad succession shortage, not a drafting-specific observation.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; true at the general small-business level, unverified for drafting services
caveats: S7 relies on older, general small-business data and does not establish an industry-specific buyer shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: I found no verified, publicly documented active consolidator focused on standalone US 541340 drafting bureaus in the reviewed public market material. The selected zero means no verified count, not proof that no buyer exists.
caveats: S5 covers broad architecture and engineering listings and transactions, not a 541340 consolidator census.
Absence of a verified public consolidator is weak evidence and should be rechecked in deal sourcing.

buy_mult.rationale: BizBuySell’s broad A&E sold-business range is 1.83–3.15x SDE, not EBITDA. I select a below-low-quartile 1.5x EBITDA buy estimate to reflect the tiny target pool and core-demand risk rather than mislabel the source’s SDE multiple as a directly comparable EBITDA observation.
caveats: S5 is broad A&E, small-business marketplace data, and seller discretionary earnings rather than 541340 EBITDA transactions.
There are no verified $1–10M EBITDA drafting-bureau comparables in the reviewed public evidence.

exit_mult.rationale: I estimate a lower exit EBITDA multiple than entry because a later buyer would underwrite more proven AI-driven pricing and demand erosion. The broad SDE market range in S5 is context only; it does not observe this exit case.
caveats: This is a forward underwriting judgment, not a prediction from observed drafting-services exits.
S5 uses broad A&E seller-discretionary-earnings multiples, so direct EBITDA comparability is poor.

pricing_structure.value: Primarily hourly, fixed-per-sheet, and fixed-scope outsourced production pricing; specialist work may be project priced.
pricing_structure.rationale: S4 directly displays hourly and fixed drawing prices for CAD services. I use it as a directional indication that production drafting is readily compared on units and hours, while recognizing a single provider cannot describe all US bureaus.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Hourly, per-sheet, and project pricing coexist; the mix by specialty is unknown.
caveats: The source is an offshore provider and does not establish the revenue mix or realized pricing of US 541340 firms.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. The high labor-savings potential reflects digital task exposure; the four-year adoption estimate remains below immediate majority adoption because implementation, client standards, QA, and workflow change slow effective use. This does not use owner age or consolidation as adoption evidence.

terminal_value.justification: Core outsourced production drafting is a specification-to-digital-documentation service [S1][S2] increasingly targeted by client-side and workflow AI [S3]. Possible field-linked or legacy niches are unquantified and may classify outside 541340, so they do not establish a durable paid-demand floor.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5413, owner aging is all-industry and old, the pricing evidence is one offshore provider, and multiples are broad A&E SDE rather than 541340 EBITDA. Each is disclosed on the affected input.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The score treats hourly/per-sheet comparability and offshore competition as a price-pass-through mechanism in pi_dist, not as task replaceability. AI can make the same production work cheaper, but the likely economic effect is a second round of bid and insourcing pressure rather than a higher operator moat.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5413, an engineering-dominated aggregate, while the target is 541340 Drafting Services. The locked shares put only 3.28% of wage share in architectural/civil drafters and leave a 48.44% residual, which is not credible as a code-level description of a pure drafting bureau.
sensitivity: If the true 541340 wage mix is more production-drafter-heavy than the locked mix, V could be higher than the finalizer’s score.
Because labor share, role weights, and residual mechanics are immutable, this packet uses only within-role judgments and flags the uncertainty rather than reweighting the dataset.
The directional capture and terminal-demand concerns do not depend on the role-mix correction.

subsegments: 2D/3D production drafting and drawing conversion from client engineering or architectural specifications
BIM documentation, revisions, and standards-driven coordination
Field capture, scan-to-BIM, and as-built measurement that may instead be classified with surveying and mapping
Engineering design, professional judgment, and seals that belong with engineering firms rather than a standalone drafting bureau
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Digital production drafting and documentation from upstream engineering or architectural specifications.
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census defines 541340 around drawings from engineering and architectural specifications [S1], and BLS describes drafters’ CAD/BIM conversion work [S2]. This selects the core production layer while explicitly excluding a claimed revenue share for field services that has not been evidenced within the code.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 5.00 at two years to 1.67 at six years; it materially changes timing but not the capture diagnosis.
V varies materially because the immutable 5413 role mix and its large residual are a poor proxy for a pure drafting bureau.
C is very sensitive to retained savings; a proved specialist, non-biddable client relationship would be the principal upside case.
B changes with a code-specific ownership census, but the frozen target count of nine remains the dominant buyability constraint.

reviewer_note: Research was deliberately constrained to live public sources and the supplied deterministic dataset. No drafting-specific adoption survey, owner-age table, consolidator census, EBITDA transaction set, or quantified surviving-niche revenue share was verified; the affected inputs are low-confidence estimates rather than fabricated observations.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
